species: Grizzly + Waterfall + Thunderstorm -- tri-layer apex intensity, remote wilderness, summer storm
scientific_name: Ursus arctos horribilis (Grizzly Bear, Ursidae) + permanent snowmelt waterfall (abiotic) + summer convective thunderstorm (meteorological)
sound_type: Tri-layer apex intensity: three simultaneous natural forces. (1) Grizzly Bear full roar: 90-100 dB at 10m, 100-300 Hz fundamental, harmonics to 1,500 Hz, 2-5 sec duration; jaw-pop warning, huffing agitation sequence; territorial/threat context. (2) Permanent snowmelt waterfall at summer maximum: 85-100 dB at 30m, broadband 20-8,000+ Hz continuous, low-frequency bias, canyon reverb reinforcement. (3) Summer thunderstorm close-range: thunder crack 120-130 dB, broadband impulse; infrasonic component below 20 Hz physically felt; 5-30 sec total thunder event. No single force reaches E=9 independently. Combined simultaneous convergence achieves E=9 through spectral completeness: all frequency bands occupied simultaneously. THE TRI-LAYER APEX INTENSITY ENTRY.
ecosystem: Remote wilderness. Subalpine montane Pacific Northwest at 5,000-7,000 feet elevation. Ambient sound level 20-30 dB (near lowest in contiguous US). North Cascades National Park (684,000 acres, 300+ glaciers) most likely specific location: confirmed Grizzly habitat, glacier-fed permanent waterfalls, summer convective storms all coexist. Multi-day backcountry access only. Grizzly Bear habitat requires mature wilderness ecosystem.
season: Summer, July-August convergence window. All three forces peak simultaneously only during this period: (1) Grizzly Bears move to subalpine zones for berries and roots; (2) snowmelt waterfalls at maximum flow from accumulated snowpack; (3) summer convective storms at peak frequency from heated terrain instability. Spring phenological pulse (April-May) handles river hydrology; summer (July-August) handles subalpine apex + storm convergence.
family: Multi-species. Ursus arctos horribilis (Order Carnivora, Family Ursidae) + permanent waterfall (abiotic, no taxonomy) + summer thunderstorm (meteorological, no taxonomy).
milestone: TRI-LAYER APEX INTENSITY. E=9 through spectral completeness: three simultaneous natural forces covering full frequency spectrum from infrasound to high-frequency. Grizzly Bear (North Cascades: fewer than 10 individuals in contiguous US population) contributes apex biological vocalization. Thunder crack at 120-130 dB is the highest amplitude sound in the SPS engine's atmospheric entries. Remote wilderness near-silence baseline (20-30 dB) amplifies dynamic range: 25 dB to 120+ dB.

coordinates.description: Representative: North Cascades National Park core wilderness, Skagit/Whatcom County WA. 48.51°N 121.02°W. Confirmed Grizzly Bear habitat (critically small population), glacier-fed waterfalls, summer convective storm exposure. Ross Lake/Cascade River/Skagit headwaters zone. Selkirk-Purcell wilderness in NE Washington also applicable.

acoustic_data.force_1_grizzly: Ursus arctos horribilis. Full roar: 90-100 dB at 10m. Frequency: 100-300 Hz fundamental, harmonics extending to 1,500 Hz. Duration: 2-5 seconds per roar. Secondary sounds: jaw-pop percussive warning signal; huffing sequence 3-5 huffs agitation. Moan: 80-200 Hz social/maternal. Roar function: territorial defense, mate competition, threat response. Simultaneously physical (felt in chest) and semantic (specific biological signal recognized by other bears and other species).
acoustic_data.force_2_waterfall: Permanent snowmelt/glacier-fed waterfall, 30-50m drop, 10-50 m³/sec summer flow. Amplitude: 85-100 dB at 30m. Spectrum: broadband 20 Hz to 8,000+ Hz, low-frequency energy concentration below 2,000 Hz. Canyon reverb maintains 70-80 dB at 100+ m. Temporal structure: continuous (unlike biological or atmospheric forces). Functions as acoustic baseline against which other two forces emerge.
acoustic_data.force_3_thunder: Summer convective thunderstorm. Lightning plasma channel 30,000 K. Thunder: crack at close range (within 300m) 120-130 dB broadband impulse; rumble at range (0.3-10 km) 1-200 Hz dominant. Infrasonic component below 20 Hz physically felt near-field. Total thunder duration per strike: 5-30 seconds. Storm wind precede cell: 30-60+ km/h. Storm frequency July-August Pacific Northwest montane: 20-40 days/year. The force that overrides all other acoustic signals at peak discharge.
acoustic_data.convergence_analysis: Spectral coverage: waterfall (20-8,000+ Hz continuous) + Grizzly (100-1,500 Hz call-specific) + thunder (infrasound-broadband impulsive). Together: near-complete spectral coverage from below 20 Hz to above 8,000 Hz. Individual force E assessments: Grizzly E=7, waterfall E=7-8, thunder E=7-8. Combined simultaneous convergence: E=9. Dynamic range from 20-30 dB ambient baseline to 120-130 dB thunder crack is the largest in any SPS wilderness entry.

nodes.title: Tri-Layer Apex Intensity -- E=9 Through Spectral Completeness
nodes.description: Three simultaneous natural forces covering full frequency spectrum: biological apex (Grizzly), hydrological broadband (waterfall), atmospheric impulse (thunder). E=9 through simultaneous coverage of all frequency bands, not through any single force. The convergence E=9 is qualitatively different from single-source E=9.

nodes.title: July-August Convergence Window
nodes.description: All three forces peak simultaneously only July-August: Grizzly in subalpine zone (berries/roots), snowmelt waterfall at maximum flow, convective storms at peak frequency. Outside this window the convergence cannot occur. A specific seasonal alignment event, not a constant condition.

nodes.title: North Cascades -- Remote Wilderness Setting
nodes.description: North Cascades NP (684,000 acres, 300+ glaciers, peaks to 9,206 ft) is the most likely location for all three conditions simultaneously. Confirmed Grizzly habitat (fewer than 10 individuals in contiguous US). Glacier-fed permanent waterfalls. Summer convective storms. Ambient 20-30 dB near-silence amplifies convergence dynamic range.

nodes.title: Grizzly Roar -- Physical and Semantic Simultaneously
nodes.description: The Grizzly roar is simultaneously felt in the chest (physical pressure from 100-300 Hz fundamental) and recognized as a specific biological threat signal. This dual quality -- physical vibration + semantic meaning -- distinguishes the biological force from the waterfall (purely physical) and thunder (purely physical but abiotic).

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. TAD's tri-layer sound (dual guitar density + bass sub-frequencies + Doyle vocal mass) against three simultaneous natural forces. Metal summit opens with structural symmetry: three musical layers vs. three natural forces.

Both E=9. Tri-layer convergence (E=9 through spectral completeness, multiple forces) vs. three consecutive breaches (E=9 through focused single-species behavioral apex). Convergence vs. concentration as two routes to maximum energy.

Apex predator encounter E=8. Grizzly tri-layer convergence E=9. Addition of two abiotic forces (waterfall, thunderstorm) extends biological apex into full environmental convergence, adding one energy unit.
